title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a repair café. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Ruth, The Sewing Table Volunteer
Ruth came to the repair café after seeing bags of torn clothes beside charity bins near her flat. [47]She was surprised that most visitors did not need complicated alterations, but simply wanted someone to show them how to sew on a button securely.[/47] Her years making theatre costumes helped her explain small stitches without making beginners feel foolish. [48]She started keeping a basket of practice fabric so nervous visitors could try the technique before touching a favourite jacket.[/48] The busiest sessions were not always the most satisfying; she preferred the quiet moments when people admitted they had been ashamed of not knowing a basic skill. Ruth says mending changed from a private chore into a conversation about waste, memory and confidence. She still refuses to call any repair invisible, because every saved garment carries a story of someone choosing not to throw it away, especially in a city that replaces things quickly.
texts.B:
### Leo, The Electronics Fixer
Leo, a retired radio engineer, was drawn to the repair café by the chance to open things up again and share tools he had kept for decades. [49]He quickly noticed that many broken lamps and kettles had failed because of one loose connection rather than a ruined part.[/49] The simplicity pleased him, but explaining safety checks to impatient owners was harder than replacing wires. [50]His most stressful repair involved a child's musical keyboard, because the family waited nearby while he searched for a fault under several layers of tiny screws.[/50] When it finally played one clear note, everyone in the room cheered. [55]Leo says volunteering forced him to describe electronics in plain English, something he had avoided during his career.[/55] He now brings a magnifying lamp and labels every plug before testing it, since one careless assumption can turn helpfulness into danger, especially when tea urns, children and trailing cables filled the same crowded room.
texts.C:
### Anika, The Welcome Desk Organiser
Anika did not repair objects herself, but the café depended on her welcome desk, a folding table near the draughty entrance. [51]Her main challenge was matching each visitor's item with the right volunteer without allowing the queue to become a competition for attention.[/51] She had managed a doctor's reception before, so she was used to calming people who felt their problem was urgent. The strangest part was seeing how emotional people became about objects that looked worthless: a cracked mug, a noisy fan, a toy train or a lamp from a first rented room. [52]She introduced short description cards after two similar toasters were accidentally returned to the wrong owners.[/52] [56]Anika says the desk taught her that organisation can be a form of kindness.[/56] She enjoys watching people leave more slowly than they arrived, often carrying the same object but with a different attitude towards it and towards their own ability to solve small problems.
texts.D:
### Mateo, The Bicycle Repairer
Mateo volunteered outside the hall, where bicycles were checked before riders joined the indoor queue and leaned their frames against the railings. [53]He had expected punctures and loose brakes on commuter bikes, but the most common problem was seats adjusted so badly that cycling had become painful.[/53] His experience as a courier helped him notice when a bike looked technically fine but uncomfortable for daily use. He enjoyed practical fixes, especially with children watching, yet he found it difficult to tell owners when a cheap helmet or cracked frame was unsafe. [54]One session changed his approach after a teenager admitted she had stopped riding to college because her chain slipped at every hill.[/54] A ten-minute adjustment gave her independence back. Mateo says repair cafés are not about nostalgia for old objects; they are about keeping people mobile, confident and less dependent on buying replacements, particularly for people whose budgets are already stretched.

questions.question: found that visitors often wanted help with a very basic repair skill?
questions.explanation: Ruth found people mainly needed help sewing on buttons securely.

questions.question: prepared spare material so people could practise before repairing a valued item?
questions.explanation: Ruth kept practice fabric for nervous visitors to try first.

questions.question: observed that many faults were caused by a simple loose connection?
questions.explanation: Leo noticed many appliances had failed because of one loose connection.

questions.question: felt pressure while repairing an object for a waiting family?
questions.explanation: Leo worked on a child's keyboard while the family waited nearby.

questions.question: had to direct people to suitable helpers while managing the queue fairly?
questions.explanation: Anika matched items with volunteers without letting the queue become competitive.

questions.question: changed the checking-in system after similar items were mixed up?
questions.explanation: Anika introduced description cards after two toasters went to the wrong owners.

questions.question: discovered that discomfort, rather than mechanical failure, was often the main issue?
questions.explanation: Mateo found badly adjusted seats were the most common problem.

questions.question: mentions a repair that allowed someone to resume an everyday journey?
questions.explanation: Mateo fixed a chain problem that had stopped a teenager cycling to college.

questions.question: says volunteering made them explain specialist knowledge in everyday language?
questions.explanation: Leo had to describe electronics in plain English.

questions.question: compares orderly administration to an act of care?
questions.explanation: Anika says organisation can be a form of kindness.
